techniques.evidence_quote: the attacker's first move was a simple GetCallerIdentity request (ATT&CK: T1078.004)
They issued GetSendQuota and GetAccount calls... (ATT&CK: T1526)
the attacker's first move was a simple GetCallerIdentity request
a clear attempt to push the SES account into production mode (ATT&CK: T1098)
creating an IAM policy named ses-support-policy and attaching it to the compromised IAM user (ATT&CK: T1098.003)
they then attempted to escalate their privileges by creating an IAM policy named ses-support-policy
adding multiple domains as verified identities... attacker-owned domains... (ATT&CK: T1583.001, T1584.001)
legitimate domains with weak DMARC protections (ATT&CK: T1583.001, T1584.001)
they created email addresses tied to these domains, using common prefixes such as: admin@, billing@, sales@, noreply@
SES abuse as a primary method for monetizing leaked AWS Access Keys
SES lets adversaries launch phishing at scale from trusted domains

attack_chain.description: Attacker obtained compromised AWS access key (exact vector unknown) and used it to authenticate to the victim AWS environment, issuing a GetCallerIdentity call which revealed 'ses-' in the key name.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker's first move was a simple GetCallerIdentity request

attack_chain.description: Attacker probed SES using GetSendQuota and GetAccount calls to determine whether the account was still in sandbox mode.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: They issued GetSendQuota and GetAccount calls, both intended to reveal the current state of the SES configuration

attack_chain.description: In a ten-second burst, attacker issued PutAccountDetails requests across all AWS regions to push SES out of sandbox into production mode; request was approved by AWS support.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a burst of PutAccountDetails requests that fanned out across all AWS regions - a strong indicator of automation and a clear attempt to push the SES account into production mode

attack_chain.description: Attacker attempted to programmatically open an AWS support case via CreateCase API to request higher SES sending limits; failed due to insufficient permissions.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: They tried to open a support ticket programmatically through the CreateCase API, asking AWS to further raise their limits

attack_chain.description: Attacker attempted privilege escalation by creating IAM policy 'ses-support-policy' and attaching it to the compromised IAM user; failed due to insufficient permissions.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: they then attempted to escalate their privileges by creating an IAM policy named ses-support-policy and attaching it to the compromised IAM user

attack_chain.description: Attacker added attacker-owned domains (managed7.com, street7news.org, street7market.net, docfilessa.com) as SES verified identities using CreateEmailIdentity API.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: adding multiple domains as verified identities using the CreateEmailIdentity API

attack_chain.description: Attacker also added legitimate third-party domains with weak DMARC protections as SES verified identities to enable spoofed phishing emails.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: legitimate domains with weak DMARC protections (which make it easier for attackers to spoof or send on behalf of the domain...)

attack_chain.description: Attacker created email addresses tied to verified domains using common prefixes (admin@, billing@, sales@, noreply@) to serve as phishing senders.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: they created email addresses tied to these domains, using common prefixes such as: admin@, billing@, sales@, noreply@

attack_chain.description: Attacker launched a phishing campaign via SES using 2024 tax-form-themed emails linking to credential theft site irss[.]securesusa[.]com behind a traffic-analysis redirect.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The phishing messages referenced 2024 tax forms... The emails linked to what we assess was a credential theft site hosted at irss[.]securesusa[.]com

impact_type: SES abuse for phishing / Resource Hijacking
initial_access_vector: Compromised/leaked AWS access key with SES permissions (exact theft vector unknown; likely public exposure in code repo or developer workstation theft)
persistence_mechanism: Manipulated SES account configuration via PutAccountDetails (moved to production mode); attempted to attach IAM policy to compromised user (failed)
